title: Reading: A Ride to the Market
learningGoal: Read and comprehend a dialogue involving hiring an auto-rickshaw, giving directions, and negotiating at a shop using formal imperatives.
instructions: Read the interaction between an American tourist (John), an auto driver, and a shopkeeper. Notice how John uses 'Bhaiya' to build rapport, but uses formal '-iye' commands to show respect.
readingTitle: बाज़ार की सैर (Trip to the Market)
readingText:
जॉन: सुनिए भैया! लाल किला चलेंगे?
ऑटो वाला: हाँ सर, बैठिए।
जॉन: भैया, आगे से दाएँ लीजिए। फिर सीधा चलिए।
ऑटो वाला: ठीक है सर। लाल किला आ गया।
जॉन: यहाँ रोकिए। कितने पैसे हुए?
ऑटो वाला: दो सौ रुपये दीजिए।
जॉन: भैया, यह बहुत ज़्यादा है! सही दाम लगाइए।
(बाज़ार में)
जॉन: भैया, यह शर्ट कितने की है?
दुकानदार: यह हज़ार रुपये की है।
जॉन: यह बहुत महँगी है। थोड़ा कम कीजिए। मुझे दो शर्ट दीजिए।
textInTargetLanguage:
जॉन: सुनिए भैया! लाल किला चलेंगे?
ऑटो वाला: हाँ सर, बैठिए।
जॉन: भैया, आगे से दाएँ लीजिए। फिर सीधा चलिए।
ऑटो वाला: ঠিক है सर। लाल किला आ गया।
जॉन: यहाँ रोकिए। कितने पैसे हुए?
ऑटो वाला: दो सौ रुपये दीजिए।
जॉन: भैया, यह बहुत ज़्यादा है! सही दाम लगाइए।
(बाज़ार में)
जॉन: भैया, यह शर्ट कितने की है?
दुकानदार: यह हज़ार रुपये की है।
जॉन: यह बहुत महँगी है। थोड़ा कम कीजिए। मुझे दो शर्ट दीजिए।
baseLanguageSupportText:
John: Excuse me brother! Will you go to Red Fort?
Auto Driver: Yes Sir, please sit.
John: Brother, take a right from ahead. Then keep going straight.
Auto Driver: Okay Sir. Red Fort has arrived.
John: Please stop here. How much money did it become?
Auto Driver: Please give two hundred rupees.
John: Brother, this is too much! Apply the correct price.
(In the market)
John: Brother, how much is this shirt for?
Shopkeeper: This is for a thousand rupees.
John: This is very expensive. Please reduce it a little. Please give me two shirts.

exampleSentence.baseTranslation: Excuse me, where is the hospital?

glossary.baseTranslation: Will you go/come? (Used to ask a driver if they will take you)

exampleSentence.baseTranslation: Will you go to India Gate?

glossary.baseTranslation: Please take (Irregular formal command of Lena)
How much money is it? (Literally: How much money happened?)

exampleSentence.baseTranslation: Brother, how much is the bill?

glossary.baseTranslation: Please give (Irregular formal command of Dena)

sentenceSupportPairs.targetText: यह बहुत महँगी है। थोड़ा कम कीजिए।
sentenceSupportPairs.transliteration: Yeh bahut mehengi hai. Thoda kam keejiye.
sentenceSupportPairs.baseTranslation: This is very expensive. Please reduce it a little.

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: What direction does John tell the auto driver to take first?
When asking for the price of the ride, what exact phrase does John use?
options: Kitne paise hue? (How much money happened?)
Kitne ka hai? (How much is this for?)
Sahi daam lagaiye. (Apply the right price.)

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: Why does John say 'Mehengi' (महँगी) instead of 'Mehenga' (महँगा) when talking about the shirt?
options: Because 'Shirt' is considered a feminine word in Hindi.
Because John is trying to be polite.

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: Identify the four formal commands John uses that end in 'iye'.
options: Suniye, Leejiye, Chaliye, Rokiye, Lagaiye, Keejiye, Deejiye

correctAnswers.rq_2: Kitne paise hue? (How much money happened?)
correctAnswers.rq_3: Because 'Shirt' is considered a feminine word in Hindi.
correctAnswers.rq_4: Suniye, Leejiye, Chaliye, Rokiye, Lagaiye, Keejiye, Deejiye

expectedSummaryPoints: Understand how to give formal directions.
Learn the stock phrases for negotiating (bahut zyada hai, thoda kam keejiye).

feedbackRules.onPass: Brilliant! You comprehended a highly authentic Indian street interaction.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review the glossary. Pay attention to all words ending in 'iye'. Those are polite commands.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort. Note that 'Kitne paise hue?' is used for services (like an auto ride or restaurant bill), while 'Kitne ka hai?' is used when pointing at an object in a shop.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: जॉन: सुनिए भैया! लाल किला चलेंगे? ऑटो वाला: हाँ सर, बैठिए। जॉन: भैया, आगे से दाएँ लीजिए। फिर सीधा चलिए। ऑटो वाला: ठीक है सर। लाल किला आ गया। जॉन: यहाँ रोकिए। कितने पैसे हुए? ऑटो वाला: दो सौ रुपये दीजिए। जॉन: भैया, यह बहुत ज़्यादा है! सही दाम लगाइए। जॉन: भैया, यह शर्ट कितने की है? दुकानदार: यह हज़ार रुपये की है। जॉन: यह बहुत महँगी है। थोड़ा कम कीजिए। मुझे दो शर्ट दीजिए।

hintText: Look closely at 'Keejiye' (Please do) and 'Deejiye' (Please give). These are the irregular forms of Karna and Dena.
commonMistakes: Thinking 'Chalenge' means 'let's go'. In this context, with a question mark, it means 'Will you go?'
